articles.title: Using a Python 3 LSP server with Python 2 code works (more or less)
articles.title_display: Python 3 LSP Still Works on Python 2 Code
articles.summary: A GNU Emacs user reports that Python 3 LSP tools like pylsp and ruff can still be useful on legacy Python 2 code. In their experience, the servers did not produce a flood of false syntax errors, handled mixed tabs-and-spaces indentation surprisingly well, and supported basics like go-to-definition and find references. The main limitation noted is that pylsp still flags Python 2 print statements as invalid Python 3 syntax, which is often a reasonable cleanup target anyway. The post is a practical compatibility note for teams that still maintain Python 2 systems and want modern editor assistance without fully porting yet.

articles.title: Bun's experimental Rust rewrite hits 99.8% test compatibility on Linux x64 glibc
articles.title_display: Bun’s Rust Rewrite Reaches 99.8% Test Compatibility
articles.summary:
Bun’s experimental Rust rewrite reportedly passes 99.8% of its pre-existing test suite on Linux x64 glibc. The post provides only a single compatibility number and no breakdown of what remains failing, which subsystems are covered, or how the rewrite performs on latency, memory use, startup time, or real workloads. There are also no benchmark comparisons against the current Bun runtime or competing JavaScript runtimes.

The signal is mainly that the rewrite is far enough along to preserve almost all existing behavior on one platform, which is useful for engineers tracking Bun’s architectural direction. But as a digest item, it is still an early status update rather than a technical deep dive, since it lacks implementation details, migration lessons, or evidence of broader production impact. It is relevant to JavaScript runtime and tooling engineers, but the practical takeaway is limited until more data is published.

articles.title: LLMs Corrupt Your Documents When You Delegate
articles.title_display: Study Finds LLM Delegates Corrupt Documents
articles.summary: This paper introduces DELEGATE-52, a benchmark for long delegated workflows across 52 professional domains, including coding, crystallography, and music notation. In a large-scale experiment covering 19 LLMs, the authors report that even frontier models such as Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude 4.6 Opus, and GPT 5.4 corrupted an average of 25% of document content by the end of long workflows. The degradation worsened with larger documents, longer interactions, and distractor files. The authors also tested agentic tool use and found that it did not improve performance on DELEGATE-52. The main takeaway is that current LLMs are not yet reliable delegates for document-heavy work: they can introduce sparse but severe errors that silently accumulate over time, which matters directly for AI-assisted editing, coding agents, and other workflow automation systems.

articles.title: How LEDs are made (2014)
articles.summary: SparkFun’s 2014 factory tour shows the end-to-end LED assembly process at YunSun: die sheets, lead-frame stamping, manual die placement, gold-wire bonding, epoxy molding, curing, lead cutting, and pogo-pin QC testing. The article includes a few concrete details, such as roughly 4,000 dies per sheet for about $12.50, manual alignment at more than 80 dies per minute, a 45-minute bake followed by 8–12 hours of curing, and the observation that the work is done in open air rather than a clean room. It also explains why LED shapes are constrained by mold design and supplier specialization across the tool chain. Useful as an accessible manufacturing walkthrough for hardware engineers, but it is primarily an educational photo essay rather than deep technical analysis.

articles.title: America's carpet capital: an empire and its toxic legacy
articles.title_display: Georgia Carpet Industry’s PFAS Pollution Legacy
articles.summary: AP’s investigative feature details how northwest Georgia’s carpet empire used PFAS-based stain treatments for decades and left a long contamination trail in rivers, wells, soil, dust, and blood. It relies on interviews, court records, internal company notes, EPA history, and blood testing from residents including Dolly Baker, Faye Jackson, Marie Jackson, and former industry worker Lisa Martin. The reporting shows that 3M warned carpet makers as early as 1998–1999 that Scotchgard chemicals were appearing in human blood and persisting in the environment, yet carpet mills continued using related PFAS formulations until 2019 in U.S. production. The story also documents regulatory failures: Dalton Utilities’ wastewater handling, EPA’s slow response, state deference, and the absence of strong federal PFAS controls for years. Measurements cited include Conasauga River samples reportedly more than 30 times proposed EPA drinking-water limits and resident blood levels ranging from 6 ng/mL to 1,336.5 ng/mL. The practical takeaway is that this is a well-sourced environmental accountability investigation, but it is not a software- or engineering-focused piece.

articles.title: All my clients wanted a carousel, now it's an AI chatbot
articles.title_display: Why Clients Keep Asking for Website Chatbots
articles.summary: The post argues that website chatbots have become a status signal rather than a tool, similar to past design fads like carousels, cookie banners, and analytics widgets. The author says clients often want a chatbot because competitors have one, even though they usually dislike using chatbots themselves and find them unhelpful. It contrasts this with fast, minimal “smolweb” sites that users often find easier to read and quicker to use, while noting that simple, well-designed sites are harder to build and easier to dismiss as “not impressive enough.” The piece is a reflective opinion rather than a technical writeup, and its main takeaway is that web design trends are driven by perceived legitimacy as much as usability.

articles.title: The Real Singularity is the Friends We Made Along the Way
articles.title_display: Geohot Argues AI Is Transformation, Not Singularity
articles.summary: This post argues that AI is a major technological shift, but not a singularity or machine-god event. The author frames current hyperscaler GPU spending as a strategic capex race driven by fear of missing out, not proof of imminent superintelligence, and compares the situation to earlier industrial transitions like steam engines and jet engines. It also rejects common doomer claims about the end of capitalism, jobs, or humanity. The piece is mostly a worldview essay rather than an engineering analysis, with no benchmarks, system details, or production evidence. Its practical value is mainly as a contrarian take on AI hype and investment narratives.
